coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive dehydroabietic acid-based difunctional fluorescent probe for detecting formaldehyde and Ag+
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Lu
Li, Mingxin
Chen, Linlin
Sun, Xuebao
Yang, Zihui
Wang, Shifa
Gu, Wen
coredata.dc:description: Formaldehyde (FA) is an environmental toxin and widely used in building and decorating materials, which is listed as a class 1 carcinogen. Silver ion (Ag+) has a beneficial bactericidal effect, while it can also cause silver poisoning and skin damage. Therefore, developing a quantitative detection technique for formaldehyde and Ag+ is necessary. In this study, a novel difunctional fluorescent probe (DBP) was designed and synthesized based on the natural product dehydroabietic acid, which could selectively detect formaldehyde and Ag+ through “turn-on” and “turn-off” fluorescence response, respectively. The probe DBP performed satisfactory nanomolar-level detection limit to formaldehyde (38 nM) and Ag+ (19 nM) in ethanol/PBS (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) solution. Furthermore, DBP could effectively recognize formaldehyde and Ag+ within a short response time (FA: 15 min, Ag+: 100 s) and a wide pH range (5–10). The sensing mechanisms of DBP to FA and Ag+ were well verified by 1H NMR titration, ESI-MS, DFT computation, and Job's plot analysis. What is more, the applications in bioimaging were carried out in MCF-7 cells and living zebrafish. Besides, the probe DBP was successfully applied in detecting FA and Ag+ in actual food and water samples, and the DBP-loaded filter paper was prepared for convenient tracking gaseous FA and Ag+.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive dehydroabietic acid-based difunctional fluorescent probe for detecting formaldehyde and Ag+

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Formaldehyde (FA) is an environmental toxin and widely used in building and decorating materials, which is listed as a class 1 carcinogen. Silver ion (Ag+) has a beneficial bactericidal effect, while it can also cause silver poisoning and skin damage. Therefore, developing a quantitative detection technique for formaldehyde and Ag+ is necessary. In this study, a novel difunctional fluorescent probe (DBP) was designed and synthesized based on the natural product dehydroabietic acid, which could selectively detect formaldehyde and Ag+ through “turn-on” and “turn-off” fluorescence response, respectively. The probe DBP performed satisfactory nanomolar-level detection limit to formaldehyde (38 nM) and Ag+ (19 nM) in ethanol/PBS (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) solution. Furthermore, DBP could effectively recognize formaldehyde and Ag+ within a short response time (FA: 15 min, Ag+: 100 s) and a wide pH range (5–10). The sensing mechanisms of DBP to FA and Ag+ were well verified by 1H NMR titration, ESI-MS, DFT computation, and Job's plot analysis. What is more, the applications in bioimaging were carried out in MCF-7 cells and living zebrafish. Besides, the probe DBP was successfully applied in detecting FA and Ag+ in actual food and water samples, and the DBP-loaded filter paper was prepared for convenient tracking gaseous FA and Ag+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Formaldehyde (FA) is one of the endogenously-produced reactive carbonyl compounds as the simplest aldehyde [1,2]. FA can maintain cell homeostasis and has the potential to monitor physiological responses due to its abundance and high reactivity [3,4]. However, high-level FA threatens human health and causes many diseases, such as heart disorders, diabetes, neurodegenerative diseases, and various cancers [5–7]. It was owing to its strong interactions with nucleic acids and proteins, which will lead to the inactivation of their biological functions [1–3]. The endogenous FA was generated from the reactions catalyzed by a series of enzymes, such as semicarbazide-sensitive amine oxidase (SSAO) [8], lysine-specific demethylase (LDS1) [9], and alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) [10]. The World Health Organization (WHO) illustrates that the tolerable daily intake (TDI) of FA is 0.15 mg kg−1 of body weight [11]. Silver, one of the precious metals with excellent thermal and electrical conductivity, possesses a natural antibacterial effect [12,13]. Thus, silver-containing materials are extensively utilized in medicine, electronics, imaging, catalyst cosmetics, building materials, and other fields owing to their unique properties [14–16]. However, The excess amount of silver ions in the water environment can cause the death of fish, shrimp, and another aquatic organisms, or eventually accumulate as a toxin in the human body to damage human health. Therefore, the human body excessively exposure to silver ions can cause many diseases, such as cardiac enlargement, growth retardation, and nerve damage, although a spot of silver ions is harmless [17–19]. The WHO has stipulated the content of silver ions in drinking water must be less than 0.5 μM [20]. Thus, it is extremely important to develop a method for sensitive and selective detection of FA and silver ions in the ecological environment.

So far, many conventional methods have been maturely applied to monitor FA and silver ion, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (ASS) [20–22], inductive coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [23], and ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) [24,25]. FA and Ag+ can be commendably detected by employing these methods. However, these methods exist various shortcomings, including sophisticated instrumentation, expensive equipment, complex sample preparation, as well as trained operators, which limit on-site detection and real-time recognition for FA and Ag+. Recently, fluorescent probe techniques have drawn increasing attention owing to their excellent sensitivity, low cost, high selectivity, easy operation, and real-time detection [26–31]. A number of fluorescent probes for the sensing of FA and Ag+ have been developed at present [32–35]. Nevertheless, these probes suffer from some imperfect disadvantages more or less and limit their applications, such as poor sensitivity, unsatisfactory detection limit, and long response time. Therefore, it is particularly pressing to develop a novel fluorescent probe with better sensing performance for the detection of FA and Ag+ in practical applications.

Nowadays, natural products are a class of abundant and renewable resources and have been widely applied to develop novel functional materials, pesticides and medicines because of their low toxicity and excellent bioactivity [36,37]. Dehydroabietic acid (DAA) is a natural diterpenic resin acid extracted from renewable forest resource rosin, which has possessed extensive biological properties, such as antitumor, antimicrobial, antiprotozoal, antiviral activities, and so on [38–43]. In recent years, dehydroabietic acid derivatives have been designed to exhibit outstanding optical properties by introducing conjugated groups and fluorophores to optimize the structure of DAA [44–46]. Nevertheless, the relevant DAA-based fluorescent probes were still seldom reported [47–49]. Therefore, continuing to develop new fluorescent probes based on DAA is still of great significance for improving the deep processing level of rosin. In this paper, a novel difunctional fluorescent probe DBP was designed and synthesized based on dehydroabietic acid for FA and Ag + detection. The probe DBP could afford high sensitivity and selectivity towards FA and Ag+, and was applied in the detection in real water and food samples as well as the bioimaging in MCF-7 cells and living zebrafish.

All reagents were analytically pure and purchased from Energy chemical (Shanghai, China). The 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were recorded by using a Bruker Avance-600 MHz spectrometer (USA) in DMSO‑d 6 solution with TMS as the internal standard. The HRMS spectra were obtained by using a Waters XEVO-G2XS-QTOF high-resolution mass spectrometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer at room temperature. The fluorescence emission spectra were tracked on a LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Melting points were monitored with an OptiMelt Automated melting point system (Stanford Research System, USA). The pH values were acquired by using Sartorius (PB-10) digital pH meter (Leica, Shanghai, China). The bioimaging experiments were carried out through the laser confocal fluorescent microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany).
The synthetic route of probe DBP was shown in Scheme 1 . Compound 2 was synthesized from dehydroabietic acid (1) according to the previously reported method [50].

About 3.0 g of compound 2 was added into a mixture solution of 19 mL fuming HNO3 and 1.5 mL concentrated H2SO4. The mixture solution was stirred for 1 h under ice bath. Subsequently, the reaction solution was poured into 200 mL ice water and extracted three times with CH2Cl2, which was concentrated and purified by a silica gel column chromatography (CC) eluted with petroleum ether/ethyl acetate (500:1–200:1, v/v) to obtain compound 3 as a yellowed solid (1.12 g, yield: 37.3%). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ 1.16 (s, 3H), 1.20 (s, 3H), 1.38 (m, 1H), 1.50 (dd, J = 13.2, 8.0 Hz, 1H), 1.55–1.82 (m, 5H), 2.04 (dd, J = 12.5, 1.8 Hz, 1H), 2.44 (dt, J = 12.7, 3.3 Hz, 1H), 2.75 (m, 1H), 2.84 (dd, J = 18.1, 6.2 Hz, 1H), 8.09 (s, 1H), 12.36 (s, 1H). HRMS: calcd for C17H19BrN2O6Na 449.0324, found [M+Na]+ 449.0336.

To a mixture solution of 30 mL toluene and 15 mL anhydrous ethanol was added compound 3 (426 mg, 1 mmol), 4-(Diphenylamino)phenylboronic acid (578 mg, 2 mmol), aqueous K2CO3 (2 mol/L, 6 mL) and Tetrakis (triphenylphosphine)palladium (115 mg, 0.2 mmol) under nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was heated to reflux at 90 °C within 12 h, and monitored by TLC. After cooling to 20 °C, the mixture was evaporated in vacuo to obtain a yellow solid, which was purified by a silica gel CC eluted with petroleum ether/ethyl acetate (100:1–10:1, v/v) to afford compound 4 (363 mg, yield: 85.4%). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ 1.18 (s, 3H), 1.25 (s, 3H), 1.43 (m, 1H), 1.52 (dd, J = 12.7, 8.0 Hz, 1H), 1.55–1.69 (m, 3H), 1.74 (m, 1H), 1.83 (m, 1H), 2.07 (dd, J = 12.7, 1.6 Hz, 1H), 2.45 (d, J = 12.5 Hz, 1H), 2.78 (m, 1H), 2.89 (dd, J = 17.9, 6.6 Hz, 1H), 6.97 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 2H), 7.08 (d, J = 7.7 Hz, 4H), 7.12 (t, J = 7.4 Hz, 2H), 7.27 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 2H), 7.35 (t, J = 7.8 Hz, 4H), 7.71 (s, 1H), 12.34 (s, 1H). HRMS: calcd for C35H34N3O6 592.2448, found [M+H]+ 592.2434.

To a solution of compound 4 (591 mg, 1 mmol) in 20 mL toluene solution was added 750 μL thionyl chloride. The reaction mixture was refluxed at 80 °C for about 6 h. After evaporation to remove solvent and residual thionyl chloride, the intermediate was dissolved in 20 mL ethyl ether at 0 °C, to which 280 μL triethylamine and 50 μL N-(3-Aminopropyl)morpholine were slowly added. The mixture was stirred overnight at room temperature. After reaction finished, the mixture was extracted with ethyl acetate and purified by silica gel CC eluted with petroleum ether/ethyl acetate (200:1–50:1, v/v) to obtain compound 5 as a yellowed solid. (473 mg, yield: 80%). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ 1.17 (s, 3H), 1.25 (s, 3H), 1.41–1.50 (m, 2H), 1.51–1.57 (m, 2H), 1.60–1.70 (m, 4H), 1.73 (m, 1H), 2.10 (dd, J = 12.7, 1.6 Hz, 1H), 2.23–2.26 (m, 2H), 2.31 (brs, 4H), 2.44 (d, J = 12.4 Hz, 1H), 2.75 (m, 1H), 2.86 (dd, J = 18.0, 6.8 Hz, 1H), 3.06 (m, 1H), 3.13 (m, 1H), 3.55 (t, J = 4.2 Hz, 4H), 6.98 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 2H), 7.08 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 4H), 7.12 (t, J = 7.4 Hz, 2H), 7.26 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 2H), 7.36 (t, J = 7.8 Hz, 4H), 7.64 (t, J = 5.2 Hz, 1H), 7.72 (s, 1H). HRMS: calcd for C42H48N5O6 718.3605, found [M+H]+ 718.3603.

To a solution of compound 5 (358 mg, 0.5 mmol) in 1,1,1,3,3,3-Hexafluoro-2-propanol (5 mL) was added iron powder (420 mg, 7.5 mmol), water (25 mL) and HCl (5 mL). The reaction mixture was stirred at 45 °C for 2.5 h. After ending, the crude product was extracted with dichloromethane, and the organic phase was washed with water, saturated NaHCO3, brine, and evaporated in vacuo. The residue was isolated using silica gel CC with petroleum ether/ethyl acetate (200:1–20:1, v/v) to yield a faint yellow solid DBP (282 mg, yield: 78.7%). M. p 120.2–121.9 °C; 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ 1.14 (s, 6H), 1.38–1.43 (m, 3H), 1.55–1.57 (m, 3H), 1.64–1.70 (m, 3H), 1.99 (d, J = 12.5 Hz, 1H), 2.18 (d, J = 12.4 Hz, 1H), 2.27 (s, 2H), 2.38–2.33 (m, 5H), 2.55–2.52 (m, 1H), 3.16–3.03 (m, 2H), 3.56 (s, 4H), 3.93 (brs, 2H), 4.17 (brs, 2H), 6.34 (s, 1H), 7.05 (dd, J = 14.0, 7.1 Hz, 8H), 7.26 (d, J = 8.4 Hz, 2H), 7.31 (t, J = 7.9 Hz, 4H), 7.61 (t, J = 5.2 Hz, 1H). 13C NMR (150 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ 14.56, 16.90, 19.06, 20.77, 21.23, 25.77, 26.29, 36.64, 36.95, 38.19, 38.62, 44.93, 46.79, 53.83, 55.37, 56.73, 60.22, 66.58, 114.29, 118.72, 123.30, 124.91, 124.25, 124.91, 129.96, 130.33, 132.52, 136.02, 140.37, 145.91, 147.73, 177.97; HRMS: calcd for C42H52N5O2 658.4121, found [M+H]+ 658.4119.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General UV–vis and fluorescence spectra measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The probe DBP was dissolved in ethanol to prepare a probe stock solution (1 mM). The stock solutions (10 mM) of various competitive analytes were prepared including the solutions of Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce4+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Cs2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Hg2+, In3+, K+, La3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, SO3 2−, ClO−, H2O2, serine (Ser), tryptophan (Trp), cysteine (Cys), L-Arg (Arg), glutathione (GSH), formaldehyde, benzaldehyde, acetone, acetaldehyde, ammonium hydroxide and methylglyoxal in distilled water. DEA·NONOate was used as a donor of nitric oxide to produce nitric oxide in situ, of which the stock solution (1 mM) was obtained by dissolving DEA·NONOate in 0.01 M NaOH/CH3CN solution. The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured at room temperature. The fluorescent emission spectra were recorded from 300 to 800 nm at λex = 325 nm with an excitation slit width 15 nm and emission slit width 2.5 nm. For the spectroscopic measurement of FA, the stock solution of DBP was diluted to 5 μM with ethanol/PBS (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) followed by adding an appropriate aliquot of FA or other competitive analytes stock solutions to 100 μM. In addition, the spectroscopic of Ag+ was measured by adding DBP (20 μM) with ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) following Ag+ or other metal ion stock solutions (100 μM). The resulting solutions were incubated for 15 min and 3 min for sensing FA and Ag+, respectively, and then the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were measured. All optical studies were operated in ethanol/PBS (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) at room temperature.

In order to further measure the optical properties of DBP, the quantum yield was calculated according to the following Eq.:

MCF-7 cells were cultured in DMEM medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (PBS), 1% penicillin, and streptomycin at 37 °C with 5% CO2. After 24 h, MCF-7 cells were transferred to 96-well microplates. Subsequently, the cells were treated by DBP with different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30 and 40 μM) for two days. After incubation at 37 °C for 36 h, MTT solution (5 mg/mL, 10 μL) was injected into each well and the cells were incubated for additional 4 h. MTT solution was then removed and the cells were washed by PBS buffer. At last, the formed blue formazan crystals were dissolved in DMSO (150 μL) and the absorbance can be obtained by a microplate reader (Thermo Fischer Scientifics, USA) to test the level of toxicity.

MCF-7 cells were cultured in DMEM medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% penicillin, and streptomycin at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then the cells were treated by the probe DBP (5 μM and 20 μM) in the 6-well flat-bottomed plates. After 30 min of incubation, the excess probes were removed and the wells were washed with PBS buffer. Subsequently, these cells were treated with different concentrations of FA and Ag+ (15 μM, 30 μM) for 30 min. Finally, the cells were washed with PBS for three times and imaged under the Laser confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany).

All experiments involving living zebrafishes and their care were performed in strict accordance with the National Care and Use of Laboratory Animals by the National Animal Research Authority (China) and guidelines of Animal Care. The living zebrafish eggs were bought from Shanghai FishBio Co., Ltd and cultured with a nutrient solution in a thermostat water bath at 26 °C. Then the juvenile fishes were put into 6-well flat-bottomed plates with deionized water. After that, part of the samples was exposed to the DBP (5 μM, 30 min) as the control group. The other samples treated with DBP (5 μM, 30 min) were further treated with FA (15 μM and 30 μM) for 30 min. For the sensing of Ag+, part of the zebrafishes was exposed to the DBP (20 μM, 30 min) as the control group. The other samples treated with DBP (20 μM, 30 min) were further treated with Ag+ (15 μM and 30 μM) for 30 min. The samples were washed three times with deionized water and anesthetized on glass slides with MS-222. Finally, the fishes were imaged under the Laser Confocal Microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FA and Ag+ in water and food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The actual environmental water samples were collected from Xuanwu Lake for the recovery experiment of DBP towards FA and Ag+. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded to analyze the FA and Ag + concentration in real water samples.

Three food samples (shrimp meat, cheese, and Basa fish fillet) were purchased from the local food market. The samples were cut into pieces and suspended in 200 mL of PBS and stirred for 4 h. When the solution became turbid, the mixed solutions were centrifuged (6000 rpm, 30 min) and collected as the extract solution. After treating with DBP (5 μM) and FA (50 μM), the fluorescence emission spectra of the food sample solutions were recorded. The measurement of the Ag+ concentration (50 μM) in Ag+-polluted food samples were conducted using a similar method with the minor modification with the concentration of DBP (20 μM).

The probe DBP was synthesized according to the route described in Scheme 1 based on natural diterpene dehydroabietic acid. The chemical structures of compounds 3–5 and DBP were characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and HRMS spectra (Figs. S1–S9).

In order to select a suitable probe concentration for spectral measurement and application experiments, the fluorescence emission spectra of DBP with different concentrations (2 μM ∼ 23 μM) in ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) were recorded. As depicted in Fig. S10, the fluorescence intensity of DBP (2–8 μM) was weak and the fluorescence of DBP (17–23 μM) was strong. Therefore, the probe concentration was set as 5 μM and 20 μM, respectively when the experiments of DBP towards FA and Ag+ were conducted. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of DBP towards various competitive analytes in ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) were measured to research the selectivity of probe DBP. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the color of DBP (5 μM) solution was transparent and had no change after adding the solution of formaldehyde. The UV–vis spectrum of DBP (5 μM) demonstrated a maximum absorption peak at 320 nm and only a slight red-shift of the absorption band (326 nm) was generated after adding FA (20 μM). It was consistent with the unchanged solution color under sunlight after the addition of FA. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 1b, the probe DBP (5 μM) solution treated with FA exhibited a higher emission peak intensity at 425 nm, but other analytes did not lead to obvious change on fluorescence emission.

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 1c, the UV–vis spectra showed that the intensity of absorption peak at 322 nm of DBP (20 μM) increased and a new absorption peak appeared at 440 nm with the addition of Ag+. Accordingly, the color of DBP (20 μM) solution was deepened and changed from colorless to brown at sunlight in the presence of Ag+. However, when other metal ions were added to the DBP solution, there was no obvious change in UV–vis spectra. Therefore, DBP could recognize Ag + ion from other cations with naked eyes at sunlight. According to the absorption values at the maximum absorption wavelength, the molar extinction coefficient of probe DBP was determined to be 7.77 × 103 mol−1 L cm−1, while the molar extinction coefficients of DBP-FA and DBP-Ag+ could be up to 1.02 × 104 mol−1 L cm−1 and 3.60 × 104 mol−1 L cm−1, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1d) indicated that only the addition of Ag + could remarkably quench the blue fluorescence of DBP (20 μM) at 425 nm in ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) under the excitation of 325 nm, whereas the influence of other metal ions to DBP was negligible. The result of fluorescence spectra clearly indicated that the probe DBP has an excellent selectivity to FA and Ag + ion. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of DBP was measured to be 21.50% in ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4). When reacted with FA and Ag+, the fluorescence quantum yields (Φ) were 66.00% and 0.23%, respectively. These results indicated that the probe DBP could detect FA and Ag + effectively.

To investigate the sensing ability of probe DBP toward FA and Ag + ion, the fluorescence titration experiments were carried out. The fluorescence emission spectra of DBP (5 μM) with the titration of different concentrations of FA (0–65 μM) and DBP (20 μM) with Ag+ (0–70 μM) in ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) was recorded in Fig. 2 a and c. As shown in Fig. 2a, the fluorescence intensity enhanced with the increment of FA concentration (0–65 nM) at 425 nm. The excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of DBP solution and the concentration (0–65 μM) of FA at 425 nm was calculated as y = 9.485x + 361.477, R2 = 0.9957 (Fig. 2b). The limit of detection (LOD) of DBP for sensing FA was obtained to be 38 nM based on formula 3δ/K, where δ is the standard deviation of measurement of blank probe sample and K is the slope of the linearity of equation [51]. As demonstrated in Fig. 2c, with the addition of Ag+, the fluorescence intensity of DBP gradually decreased. Fig. 2d exhibited the good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of DBP solution at 425 nm and the concentration (0–70 μM) of Ag+ (y = −14.756x + 1032.165, R2 = 0.9974). The limit of detection (LOD) of DBP for sensing Ag+ was calculated to be 19 nM. The nanomolar-level LOD of DBP toward Ag+ is much lower than the stipulated level (0.5 μM) by WHO in drinking water. The above results indicated that the probe DBP possessed high sensitivity and ultralow LOD for detecting FA and Ag+.

The interference of other analytes to FA and Ag+ should be taken into account when the fluorescent probe was used in the practical application. Hence, the competition experiment was performed to evaluate the anti-interference ability of DBP. The fluorescence intensity of DBP (5 μM) was detected by adding 100 μM of other competitive analytes (1. Mg2+, 2. Zn2+, 3. SO3 2−, 4. ClO−, 5. H2O2, 6. Ser, 7. Trp, 8. Cys, 9. Arg, 10. GSH, 11. Benzaldehyde, 12. Acetone, 13. Acetaldehyde, 14. Ammonium hydroxide, 15. NO and 16. Methylglyoxal) to the ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) solutions in the absence and presence of FA (100 μM). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of DBP (20 μM) was investigated by adding 100 μM of other metal ions (1. Ca2+, 2. Cd2+, 3. Ce4+, 4. Cr3+, 5. Co2+, 6. Cr6+, 7. Na+, 8. Cs2+, 9. Cu+, 10. Cu2+, 11. Fe2+, 12. Fe3+, 13. Zn2+, 14. Hg2+, 15. In3+, 16. K+, 17. La3+, 18. Mn2+, 19. Mg2+, 20. Ni2+, 21. Pb2+, 22. Sn2+) in the absence and presence of Ag+ (100 μM). As shown in Fig. S11a, the changes of F/F0 were negligible under the addition of competitive analytes, while it increased obviously under the addition of FA. As expected, in Fig. S11b, (F0–F)/F0 were about 0.9 under the addition of other ions while dropped under 0.1 after simultaneously adding Ag+. In consequence, the result suggested that probe DBP could selectively detect FA and Ag+, and not suffer interference from other competitive analytes.

Response time is also an important feature to evaluate the detection ability of fluorescent probes [52]. The rapid response time greatly improves the detection efficiency of the fluorescent probe. To study the response time of probe DBP against FA and Ag+ in ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) solution, the fluorescence intensities of DBP at 425 nm were recorded in the presence of FA and Ag+, respectively. As shown in Fig. S12, the fluorescent intensity of DBP (5 μM) kept a stable tendency. After adding FA (100 μM), the emission intensity increased rapidly and reached a steady state within 15 min. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of DBP (20 μM) dropped sharply within 10 s upon treatment with Ag+ in Fig. S13. As the response time went on, the fluorescence intensity gradually reached stabilization within 100 s. Therefore, it could be concluded that the probe DBP could quickly monitor FA within 15 min and Ag+ within 100 s.

The effect of pH is a critical parameter that can determine the practicality of a fluorescent probe [53]. In order to investigate the appropriate pH range of probe DBP towards FA and Ag+ in practical application, the ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4) solvents with different pH values ranging from 2 to 13 were prepared by adding HCl and NaOH. The fluorescence intensities of probe DBP (5 μM) at 425 nm with or without FA (100 μM) and DBP (20 μM) at 425 nm with or without Ag+ (100 μM) at various pH values were recorded in the above-mentioned solutions. As shown in Figs. S14 and S15, DBP exhibited satisfactory sensing ability in the pH range from 5 to 10 after being treated with FA and Ag+. However, the differences of emission intensity of DBP with and without FA and Ag + at pH < 5 and pH > 10 were not conspicuous. Hence, these data indicated that DBP could effectively recognize FA and Ag+ in a broad pH range of 5–10.

The sensing mechanisms of DBP for selectively recognizing FA and Ag+ were explored. First of all, the 1H NMR titration experiment of DBP in DMSO‑d 6 was carried out to confirm the reactive mode between DBP and FA. As depicted in Fig. S16, with the addition of 0–2 equivalent formaldehyde, the proton peaks at δ 3.95 ppm and 4.18 ppm corresponding to the two –NH2 groups gradually weakened and finally disappeared. Meanwhile, the proton signals at δ 10.22 ppm and δ 12.42 ppm emerged and grew. It was due to the reaction of two –NH2 groups with FA, which formed an imidazole ring. The singlet at δ 10.22 ppm and the broad singlet at δ 12.42 ppm could be assigned to the aromatic C–H and the N–H in the imidazole ring, respectively. Fig. S17 showed the mass peak at m/z = 668.3918 belonging to the quasimolecular peak of the reaction product [DBP + FA + H]+, ([C43H50N5O2]+, calcd. 668.3965), which coincided well with the results exhibited by 1H NMR titration analysis. Therefore, the 1H NMR and HRMS analyses strongly proved the reaction mechanism of DBP towards FA.

The coordination ratio between DBP and Ag+ was detected through the Job's plot experiment. As shown in Fig. S18, the intersection point of the two linear fitting curves was approximately 0.52 implying that the coordination ratio of DBP with Ag+ was probably 1:1. Moreover, the 1H NMR titration experiment of DBP in DMSO‑d 6 was carried out to confirm the coordination behavior between DBP and Ag+. As displayed in Fig. S19, the two signals at δ 4.18 ppm and δ 3.95 ppm corresponding to the protons of two –NH2 groups occurred changes of chemical shifts to high field with the increase of Ag + concentration. When 5 equiv. of Ag+ was added into the solution, the signals of two groups were observed at δ 3.83 ppm and δ 1.77 ppm, which implied that Ag+ was probably complexed with the two –NH2 groups of DBP. Meanwhile, the ESI-MS spectrum of the complex DBP-Ag+ further confirmed the coordination result, which indicated that DBP formed a 1:1 complex with Ag + due to the mass peak at m/z = 764.3091 belonging to [DBP + Ag]
+ ([C42H51N5O2Ag]+, calcd 764.3094) as shown in Fig. S20.

In summary, based on a comprehensive analysis of the results including 1H NMR titration analysis, HRMS spectrum, and Job's plot experiment, the sensing mechanisms of DBP toward FA and Ag+ were proposed as shown in Scheme 2 .

In order to better understand the sensing mechanism with regard to coordination. The density functional theory (DFT) was performed to study the molecular configuration and electron cloud distribution of DBP, DBP-FA, and DBP-Ag+ complex. First of all, the structure of DBP, DBP-FA, and DBP-Ag+ were optimized by Gaussian 09 program with B3LYP and 6-311G (d, p) method, and the HOMO and LUMO were calculated to determine the electron distribution and orbital energies. As the result shown in Fig. S21, the HOMO-LUMO energy gaps (ΔE) calculated for DBP, DBP-FA, and DBP-Ag+ were 4.295 eV, 4.062 eV, and 3.166 eV, respectively. The reduction of ΔE stabilized the whole molecular system, and confirmed the reasonability of coordination behavior. The HOMO and LUMO of DBP were both mainly distributed in triphenylamine group, so the DBP emitted blue fluorescence. The HOMO of DBP-FA complex was mainly situated in triphenylamine group, while the LUMO mainly located in the benzimidazole moiety. Therefore, the blue fluorescence intensity of probe DBP became stronger due to the transfer of electrons from the excited fluorophore to the donor. However, after DBP reacted with Ag+, the HOMO of DBP-Ag+ complex was mainly situated in triphenylamine group, while the LUMO was mainly located in the coordination sites of DBP and Ag+. The decreased ΔE from 4.295 to 3.166 eV was also consistence with the UV–vis absorbance change.

The cytotoxic effect of DBP on MCF-7 cells were evaluated by the MTT test. As shown in Fig. S22, the probe DBP showed very mild cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells. The low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of DBP implied that the probe could be applied for cell imaging. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the blue fluorescence could hardly be seen when the MCF-7 cells were treated with DBP (5 μM) for 30 min, while the fluorescence intensity in the blue channel enhanced gradually with the increased concentration of FA (0–30 μM). On the contrary, as depicted in Fig. 3b, MCF-7 cells exhibited strong fluorescence in the blue channel after treatment with DBP (20 μM) for 30 min. When the cells were further treated with Ag+ (0–30 μM), the fluorescence intensity gradually reduced until the fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the probe DBP could be successfully applied in the detection of FA and Ag+ in MCF-7 cells.

To evaluate the sensing capability of DBP in living organisms, the fluorescent imaging experiment of DBP towards FA and Ag+ was further carried out in living zebrafish. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, weak blue fluorescence could be seen in zebrafishes stained with DBP (5 μM) for 30 min. However, an increased blue fluorescence was observed in zebrafishes which were further treated with an incremental concentration of FA. Furthermore, as described in Fig. 4b, strong fluorescence in the blue channel could be seen in zebrafishes after treatment with DBP (20 μM) for 30 min, when the zebrafishes were further treated with Ag+ (0, 15 and 30 μM), the fluorescence intensity gradually reduced and finally quenched. The results manifested that the DBP could detect FA and Ag+ in living zebrafish.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Filter paper test of gaseous FA and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: To monitor gaseous FA and Ag + by DBP, a convenient filter paper detection was developed. As described in Fig. 5 a, the filter paper strips were immersed in the ethanol solution of DBP (5 μM) for 1 h and taken out to evaporate the solvent. Then the filter paper strips were put into glass sample bottle and suspended above the FA solutions from 0 μM to 50 μM for about 1 h. The fluorescence of filter paper strips changed from colorless to bright blue under a 365 nm UV lamp. Furthermore, the DBP-based test strip was prepared by infiltrating the filter paper in an ethanol solution of DBP (20 μM) and dried. Subsequently, the different concentrations water solutions of Ag+ were dropped onto the DBP-based test strips, which was dried at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 5b, the DBP-based test strip exhibited a bright blue fluorescence without treatment with Ag+ solution under 365 nm UV light, and the color of filter paper did not changed under sunlight. However, the bright blue fluorescence quenched gradually when the DBP-based test strips were treated with Ag+ solution. Meanwhile, the obvious color change in test paper from colorless to brown was easily observed with naked eyes. The results revealed that the probe DBP can detect gaseous FA and Ag + through a probe-loaded filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FA and Ag+ in food and water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The detection ability of probe DBP to FA and Ag+ in actual food samples was investigated. We extracted aqueous solutions of three commercially available food samples (shrimp meat, cheese, and Basa fish fillet) purchased from the supermarket nearby as typical examples. As can be seen from Fig. 6 b, the fluorescence emission intensity of normal samples treated with DBP (5 μM) at 425 nm was much lower than the solution-contaminated samples (100 μL) and FA (50 μM) after treatment with DBP (5 μM). Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission intensity of normal samples treated with DBP (20 μM) was higher than solution treated with food samples (100 μL), DBP (20 μM) and Ag+ (50 μM). It indicated that the levels of FA and Ag+ in the normal food were almost negligible and the probe DBP has the potential to detect FA and Ag+ in food samples.

Fluorescent probe that can be applied to detect metal ions in real samples is greatly desirable. Further experiments were carried out to evaluate the practicality of probe DBP toward FA and Ag+ in real water samples from Xuanwu Lake and Zihu Creek. The fluorescence emission spectra of DBP (5 μM) with different amounts of FA (0, 10, 20, and 30 μM) and DBP (20 μM) with different amounts of Ag+ (0, 10, 20, and 30 μM) were detected and recorded in conjunction with a standard addition method. The recovery results of FA and Ag+ in water samples were calculated. As displayed in Table S1, the experimental results showed good fluorescence recoveries for FA and Ag+ detection, which indicated that probe DBP could be effectively applied to detect FA and Ag+ in actual environmental water samples.

In summary, a novel turn-on and turn-off difunctional fluorescent probe DBP based on the natural product dehydroabietic acid was designed and synthesized for the detection of formaldehyde and Ag+. The probe DBP performed high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for formaldehyde and Ag + over other competitive analytes in ethanol/PBS buffer (3/7, v/v, pH = 7.4, 10 mM). Meanwhile, compared with other fluorescents probe that have been reported for FA and Ag+ (Table S2), DBP possessed many fascinating detection performances including rapidly recognizing FA and Ag+, wide pH response range (5–10), and low detection limits of DBP to FA (38 nM) and Ag+ (19 nM). In addition, the probe DBP was successfully applied in fast detection of gaseous FA and Ag + through DBP-loaded filter paper, detection of FA and Ag+ in food and water samples, and biological imaging in MCF-7 cells and living zebrafishes.

Lu Sun: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mingxin Li: Investigation, Writing – review and editing. Linlin Chen: Formal analysis. Xuebao Sun: Software. Zihui Yang: Investigation.
